title:
Nota Bene

Type A Aortic Dissection / Pearls
Pain ...
original_text:
Nota Bene

Type A Aortic Dissection / Pearls
Pain is your enemy
Activates sympathetic nervous system driving tachycardia and hypertension that increases aortic wall stress
ACC/AHA: pain control is essential for hemodynamic management
Time is death 
Mortality approaches 1-2% per hour if not rapidly identified and treated
Prioritize rapid transport over perfect hemodynamic optimization
Hypotension is ominous
Causes: tamponade, aortic rupture, severe aortic regurgitation, myocardial ischemia
Bedside Assessment
Bilateral arm BPs are essential
Asymmetric BP > 20 mmHg = branch artery compromise
Present in ~30% of patients
Not specific/not sensitive, essential for treatment
Listen for a new murmur
Aortic regurgitation present in ~32% of Type A dissections
Indicates root involvement
Caution with beta blockers - they block compensatory tachycardia
Left sternal border (3rd-4th intercostal spaces); patient sitting forward; end-expiratory hold; high pitched, blowing; loudest at start, fades through diastole; long duration = more severe
Wide pulse pressure (> 50% of SBP) suggests significant AR
Quincke’s pulse: nail bed capillary pulsations - press gently on nail
Hill’s sign: leg SBP > 30 mmHg higher thn arm SBP
Water-hammer pulse: bounding radial pulse
Bedside ultrasound is highly valuable
Look for mobile intimal flap separating true and false lumens
False lumen is typically larger with slower, duller flow on color Doppler
Parasternal long-axis: diastolic fluttering of anterior mitral leaflet or color Doppler regurgitant jet
Recognize malperfusion syndromes:
Limb ischemia: absent pulses, mottle extremities
Mesenteric ischemia: abdominal pain, elevated lactate
Renal malperfusion: oliguria, hematuria
Spinal cord ischemia: paraplegia
Stroke: carotid involvement, neurological deficits
Esmolol:
Ultra short half life = 9 minutes
ED Study: 82.5% achieved lenient HR control (≤80 bpm) within 60 minutes; 27.5% achieved strict control (≤ 60 bpm); hypotension in 12.5%.
Ketamine / Hemodynamics
Central mechanisms:
Central sympathetic stimulation: 
Direct CNS action → ↑ sympathetic outflow → ↑ heart rate, blood pressure, cardiac output
Catecholamine reuptake inhibition:
Blocks neuronal norepinephrine reuptake at neuroeffector junction → ↑ norepinephrine at synaptic cleft → β-adrenoreceptor activation → positive inotropy
Sympathoneuronal + sympathoadrenal activation:
Plasma norepinephrine ↑  ~120% (187→415 ng/L), epinephrine ↑ 180% (97→271 ng/L)
Locus coeruleus activation:
↑  norepinephrine neuron firing and burst activity
Peripheral/Direct Myocardial Effects
Direct negative inotropy (unmasked when sympathetic system blocked: 
↓ transsarcolemmal Ca2+ influx → ↓ intracellular Ca2+ availability → ↓ contractility
L-type Ca2+ channel inhibition:
Reduces Ca2+ current (ICa) → shortens action potential duration
Dose-dependent:
Low concentrations may show positive inotropy (S(+)-ketamine; high concentrations → negative inotropy)
Net hemodynamic effects:
↑ heart rate (~28%)
↑ systolic BP (~28%), diastolic BP (~17%)
↑ cardiac output (in patients with intact sympathetic tone)
↑ myocardial oxygen demand
Simple summary:
Indirect (dominant): central sympathetic activation + catecholamine reuptake inhibition → ↑ HR, ↑ BP, ↑ CO
Direct (masked): negative inotropy via ↓  Ca2+ influx (becomes clinically relevant in catecholamine depleted or severely failing hearts)

Midazolam / Sedation Bolus / Sympatholysis
Central mechanisms:
GABA-A receptor potentiation: 
binds to benzodiazepine site (between α and ɣ subunits) → ↑ chloride channel opening frequency when GABA binds → enhanced CNS inhibition
α1-subunit predominant: 
sedative/amnestic effects primarily mediated through α1-GABA-A receptor subtype
Baroreflex depression: 
↓ baroreflex sensitivity by ~45% → impaired compensatory response to hypotension
Central vagal tone reduction:
↓ high-frequency heart rate variability (vagal marker) → contributes to initial heart rate increase
Sympathetic suppression:
↓ plasma norepinephrine and epinephrine → sustained decrease in sympathetic tone
Peripheral mechanisms:
Inhibits norepinephrine release:
At nerve terminals → ↓ neurotransmitter release during sympathetic stimulation
α1 and α2 -adrenoreceptor blockade:
Direct attenuation of adrenergic receptor-mediated vasoconstriction
↓ Systemic vascular resistance:
Direct vascular smooth muscle relaxation ↓ vasodilation
Net hemodynamic effects:
↓ mean arterial pressure (slight to moderate)
↓ cardiac output and stroke volume
↓ systemic vascular resistance
Heart rate: slow rates tend to ↑ slightly; fast rates tend to ↓ slightly
Simple Summary
Central: GABA-A potentiation → ↓ sympathetic outflow + ↓ baroreflex + ↓ vagal tone
Peripheral: ↓ norepinephrine release + α-adrenergic blockade → vasodilation
Net effect: mild hypotension (less than propofol) + variable HR changes

Fentanyl / Sympatholysis
Central mechanisms;
Brainstem sympathetic centers:
μ-opioid receptor activation → ↓ sympathetic outflow → ↓ vascular tone → hypotension
Vagal nuclei:
 μ-opioid receptor activation → removes GABA inhibition of vagal neurons → ↑ parasympathetic tone → bradycardia
Baroreflex enhancement:
Amplifies vagal response to blood pressure changes
Peripheral mechanisms
α1-adrenoreceptor blockade: direct vasodilation (unique to fentanyl vs morphine)
Peripheral opioid receptors: contribute ~50% of immediate bradycardia
Key takeaways:
Fentanyl = central vagal activation + central sympathetic inhibition + peripheral α1-blockade
Simple summary: 
↑ vagal tone + ↓ sympathetic tone + direct vasodilation = bradycardia + hypotension

Atrial Fibrillation with RVR / Stepwise Approach
Unstable → Cardioversion
200J
Anterior-Lateral pad placement
Stable, EF > 40% → IV metoprolol or IV diltiazem ± IV magnesium (concurrent)
CAD: Beta blocker
COPD/Asthma: Diltiazem/Verapamil
Hypotension concern: Esmolol; avoid diltiazem
Need faster rate control: IV magnesium
Low adverse effect profile; synergistic with AV nodal blockers
Stable, EF < 40% or decompensated HF → IV metoprolol (cautiously) ± digoxin; avoid CCBs
Inadequate response to Steps 2-3 → Add digoxin (if not already given)
Critically ill/Refractory despite above → IV amiodarone
Decompensated HF with refractory RVR
Critically examples:
ICU patients with hemodynamic instability
Cannot tolerate diltiazem due to hypotension
Decompensated HF
Patients with HFrEF
Patients on vasopressors who cannot tolerate additional negative inotropy
Pre-excitation present → Cardioversion (avoid all AV nodal blockers)

Amiodarone / Acute IV Effects
AV Nodal:
Prolonged intranodal conduction 
Due to slow calcium channel blockade (class IV activity)
Increased AV nodal refractoriness
Due to calcium channel blockade and noncompetitive adrenergic antagonism
Occur before significant tissue accumulation or formation of active metabolite
Calcium channel mechanisms:
Use dependent L-type voltage gated calcium channels
Frequency dependent augmentation of AV nodal effects
Adrenergic antagonism mechanisms:
Noncompetitive sympatholytic action
Induces partial norepinephrine depletion in the heart by interfering with vesicular NE storage and enhancing intraneuronal NE metabolism
Dose-dependent increase in dihydroxyphenylglycol (DHPG), the intraneuronal metabolite of NE 
Impairs NE release during sympathetic activation
Works synergistically with calcium channel blockade to produce negative dromotropic effects on both sinus and AV nodes. 
No significant acute effect on:
Sinus cycle length: negative chronotropic effects minimal acutely
QTc interval: class III effects (action potential prolongation) require prolonged administration
QRS duration: sodium channel blockade occurs primarily at rapid pacing frequencies
Atrial refractoriness
Ventricular refractoriness
Infranodal conduction
Acute Hemodynamic Changes and Mechanisms
Vasodilation: decreases cardiac workload and myocardial oxygen consumption
Early and usually short reduction of systemic and coronary vascular resistance
Partially due to vasodilating properties of solvent (polysorbate 80)
Decreases afterload and cardiac work
Increases CA and coronary blood flow
Early vasodilation is followed by late negative inotropic effects
Contractility is reduced at the end of amiodarone administration and remains depressed after infusion
Negative inotropic effects
Hypotension
Myocardial oxygen demand and consumption do not change
Dose dependent effects
Relevant at higher infusions rates
Ventricular refractoriness prolongation and QRS widening (class III and class I) manifest with greater drug exposure even in acute setting

Norepinephrine / Cardiac Output
Does not decrease cardiac output at specific threshold.
Effect on cardiac output is variable and patient dependent
Influenced by:
Baseline cardiac function
Volume status
Balance between afterload and enhanced venous return
Pharmacodynamic effects include:
Vasoconstriction (increasing afterload)
Cardiac stimulation
Reflex vagal activity that slows heart rate
Increases stroke volume
Mechanism of reduced cardiac output
Excessive increases in afterload (SVR) outweigh the benefits of increases mean systemic filling pressure and venous return
Particularly a problem in hypovolemic patients or those with impaired cardiac function who cannot overcome increased afterload. 
Baseline stroke volume variation predicted cardiac output response
SVV > 8.7% more likely to experience increased CO
Suggests better preload responsiveness and cardiac reserve

Norepinephrine Receptor Selectivity
Maintains high α-1 adrenergic receptor selectivity compared to β-2 adrenergic receptor stimulation at doses up to 50 mcg/min
Potent α-1 and β-1 agonist with minimal β-2 activity throughout the clinical dosing range.
β-2 receptor stimulation requires concentrations approximately 10-fold higher than those needed for β-1 activation

Differentiating a Laryngectomy vs. Tracheostomy
Primary Physical Exam Findings
Absence of the laryngeal prominence ("Adam's apple"):
In total laryngectomy, the entire larynx (including thyroid cartilage) is surgically removed.
Palpation of the anterior neck should reveal absence of the thyroid cartilage/laryngeal prominence in laryngectomy patients, whereas tracheostomy patients retain this structure.
Stoma characteristics:
Laryngectomy stoma:
Typically a permanent, sutured opening (end stoma) where the trachea is brought directly to the skin surface. It is often larger, more mature-appearing, and may have a heat moisture exchanger (HME) device or adhesive baseplate attached rather than a tube.
Tracheostomy:
Usually has a tube in place (tracheostomy tube with flange, cuff, and inner cannula) secured with ties or sutures. The tube passes through an incision into an intact trachea.
Upper airway patency test: Occlude the stoma and observe for air movement through the nose/mouth. 
In laryngectomy patients, there will be NO air movement through the upper airway because the respiratory and digestive tracts are permanently separated.
In tracheostomy patients (unless completely obstructed above), some air movement may be detected.
Stoma location: 
Laryngectomy stomas are typically located lower in the neck (at the suprasternal notch level) compared to tracheostomies, which are usually higher.
Practical Approach in Emergency
If uncertain, assume the patient is a total neck breather and ventilate through the stoma only. 
The 2025 AHA Guidelines recommend using a pediatric face mask over the stoma to create a tight seal for bag-mask ventilation.
If the patient has a tracheostomy tube in place, this strongly suggests tracheostomy rather than laryngectomy (though some laryngectomy patients may wear a laryngectomy tube or stoma button).
Key Differentiators on Physical Exam
Laryngeal Prominence
Laryngectomy: Absent (surgically removed)
Tracheostomy: Present
Tube in Stoma
Laryngectomy: Usually none (may have stoma button/HME)
Tracheostomy: Usually has tracheostomy tube
Stoma Appearance
Laryngectomy: Sutured, mature, end stoma
Tracheostomy: Incision with tube through intact trachea
Upper Airway Patency
Laryngectomy: None
Tracheostomy: Usually some patency
Stoma Location
Laryngectomy: Lower (suprasternal notch). Entire larynx (including thyroid cartilage, cricoid cartilage, and upper tracheal rings) is surgically removed. The remaining trachea is then brought forward and sutured directly to the skin of the anterior neck. Because the larynx and upper tracheal rings have been excised, the resulting stoma is created from the remaining distal trachea, which anatomically sits lower in the neck - typically at or near the suprasternal notch.
Tracheostomy: Higher in neck. The larynx remains intact. Involves making an incision into an intact trachea, typically through the 2nd to 4th rings, which are located higher in the neck (below the cricoid cartilage but well above the sternal notch. The trachea is not transected or brought to the skin surface - rather, a tube is inserted through the incision into the existing tracheal lumen.

Ketamine / Eclampsia 
For an extremely hypertensive eclamptic patient, ketamine at a reduced dose (0.5 - 1 mg/kg) combined with aggressive adjunctive blood pressure control is likely the best choice, despite its sympathomimetic properties.
A network meta-analysis specifically examining hypertensive parturients found that labetalol and remifentanil were most effective at blunting the hypertensive response to intubation.

Adrenal suppression is particularly problematic, where neonatal adrenal function is critical for extrauterine transition.
Why Not Midazolam:
Longer onset of action.
Potent venodilator at induction doses.

It is the least suitable option for RSI.

Pre-treat with IV labetalol (10 - 20 mg) or have it ready for immediate post-intubation administration.
Use ketamine at a reduced dose (0.5 - 1 mg/kg IV) to minimize sympathomimetic effects while still achieving adequate induction.
Ensure magnesium sulfate is running for seizure prophylaxis.

Fentanyl / Hypotension (From FDA)
Fentanyl causes hypotension through multiple distinct mechanisms: 
Central vagal activation producing bradycardia 
Direct peripheral vasodilation
Impaired compensatory responses
Endocrine dysfunction
Central vagal stimulation via the nucleus ambiguus 
Increases parasympathetic outflow to the heart, causing bradycardia that reduces cardiac output and blood pressure. 
This mechanism is mediated by µ-opioid receptor activation on cardiac vagal neurons and can be nearly abolished by vagotomy.
Direct peripheral vasodilation
Primary cardiovascular effect of fentanyl that occurs independent of histamine release.
The mechanism involves direct effects on vascular smooth muscle, including competitive alpha-adrenergic receptor blockade (approximately 1/30 the potency of phentolamine) and attenuation of norepinephrine-induced phosphatidylinositol signaling at concentrations of 1-10 μM.
Impaired compensatory vasoconstriction occurs 
Through inhibition of α-adrenergic signaling pathways.
Fentanyl attenuates the norepinephrine-induced phosphatidylinositol response essential for α-1 adrenoceptor-mediated vasoconstriction
Acts as a reversible competitive alpha-adrenergic antagonist. 
In hemorrhage models, compensatory arteriolar vasoconstriction is completely abolished, replaced by sustained vasodilation.
Reduced blood volume or concurrent CNS depressants (phenothiazines, general anesthetics) significantly increase hypotension risk.
In patients with circulatory shock, fentanyl-induced vasodilation can further reduce cardiac output and blood pressure.
Endocrine dysfunction may contribute through inhibition of adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) and cortisol secretion. 
Prolonged opioid use can cause adrenal insufficiency, presenting with hypotension among other symptoms.
This mechanism becomes relevant with extended use rather than acute administration.
Fentanyl induced hypotension is primarily mediated through µ-opioid receptor activation causing peripheral vasodilation, rather than through a specific receptor subunit. 
The µ-receptor itself is responsible for the vasodilatory effects.
Fentanyl also reversibly blocks alpha-adrenergic receptors in a competitive manner in vascular smooth muscle.
The alpha blocking effect is not reversed by naloxone, distinguishing it from opioid receptor mediated effects.
Attenuates α1B-adrenoreceptor-mediated vasoconstriction
Two main mechanisms of fentanyl induced hypotension:
1) µ-receptor mediated vasodilation (primary mechanism described in FDA labeling and cardiology literature)
Opioid receptors are present in the cardiovascular system, including heart and vasculature.
µ-receptor activation likely triggers intracellular signaling pathways that affect vascular smooth muscle.
2) Direct α1-adrenergic receptor blockade (an off-target effect not shared by morphine)

Dobutamine / Inotropy / Lusitropy
Dobutamine increases inotropy and lusitropy through β-adrenergic receptor stimulation
Which activates a cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP)-dependent cascade that enhances intracellular calcium handling.
When dobutamine binds to β1- and β2-adrenergic receptors on cardiomyocytes, it stimulates adenylyl cyclase to convert ATP into cAMP.
The elevated cAMP activates protein kinase A (PKA), which phosphorylates several key downstream targets that mediate both contractile and relaxation effects.
For inotropy (increased contractile force), PKA phosphorylation acts on:
Ryanodine receptors on the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR), increasing calcium release during depolarization
Troponin C, facilitating actin exposure for myosin binding
Cardiac myosin binding protein-C (MyBP-C), which is the principal mediator of the contractile response to β-agonist stimulation
For lusitropy (enhanced relaxation), PKA phosphorylation targets:
Phospholamban, which relieves its inhibition of SERCA2a (SR calcium-ATPase), thereby accelerating calcium reuptake into the SR
This increased SR calcium-ATPase activity shortens the time constant of relaxation (tau) and accelerates the rate of left ventricular pressure decline (-dP/dt)
Lusitropic response to dobutamine is well preserved even in heart failure, despite substantial attenuation of the inotropic response.
This occurs because the sensitivity of calcium-ATPase activity to cAMP is increased in failing hearts, contributing to maintained positive lusitropy

Dobutamine / Hemodynamic Benefits of Increased Lusitropy
Reduces left ventricular end diastolic pressure and enhances ventricular filling
Increases stroke volume and cardiac output without proportional increases in heart rate
Accelerates isovolumetric relaxation which allows the ventricle to fill more completely during diastole at lower filling pressures
Reduction in LVEDP decreases pulmonary venous congestion
Optimizes preload for the subsequent contraction
Lusitropic effects occur at lower doses than the inotropic rate
5 mcg/kg/min: significant increases in peak filling rate without changing ejection fraction or heart rate
Suggests that enhanced relaxation contributes to improved cardiac output even before contractility increases substantially.
Inotropic effects become apparent at 10 mcg/kg/min



Dobutamine / L-Type Voltage-Gated Calcium Channels
L-type voltage-gated calcium channels (LTCCs) are involved in dobutamine's mechanism of action, but they play a secondary role rather than being the primary target of PKA phosphorylation.
When dobutamine activates β-adrenergic receptors and increases cAMP/PKA signaling, PKA does phosphorylate L-type calcium channels.
This phosphorylation induces conformational changes that promote calcium flux into the cytosol, contributing to increased contractility.
The enhanced LTCC activity facilitates calcium influx during the action potential plateau phase, which then triggers calcium-induced calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum via ryanodine receptors.

Dobutamine / Adrenergic Receptors
β-adrenergic receptor stimulation and increased cAMP mechanism is responsible for the modest increase in heart rate observed with dobutamine, though this chronotropic effect is comparatively mild compared to other catecholamines like isoproterenol. 
The increase in heart rate occurs through β1-receptor stimulation in the sinoatrial node, with the cAMP/PKA pathway enhancing automaticity. 
Vasodilation and decreased pulmonary vascular resistance result from β2-adrenergic receptor stimulation in the peripheral and pulmonary vasculature, combined with reflex withdrawal of intense vasoconstriction. 
The β2-receptor activation in vascular smooth muscle increases cAMP, leading to relaxation and reduced systemic vascular resistance.
 Additionally, dobutamine appears to act as an α-adrenergic receptor antagonist in the peripheral vasculature, which contributes to its vasodilatory properties independent of β2-stimulation.
In the pulmonary circulation, dobutamine at doses up to 10 μg/kg/min has minimal flow-independent effects on pulmonary vascular resistance in normal conditions, though higher doses (20 μg/kg/min) may produce pulmonary vasodilation in the setting of acute pulmonary hypertension.
The vasodilatory effects are more prominent systemically than in the pulmonary vasculature.

Ketamine / Mechanism of Action
Ketamine binds within the central vestibule of the NMDA receptor channel, positioned between the channel gate and selectivity filter.
Where it acts as a non-competitive, use-dependent channel blocker.
The drug functions through multiple distinct biochemical mechanisms at the molecular level.
At the receptor level:
Ketamine occludes the open channel pore, requiring channel activation before it can access its binding site.
Structural studies have identified the precise binding pocket.
Molecular dynamics simulations demonstrate that ketamine moves between two distinct locations within this binding pocket.
The blockade occurs through dual mechanisms:
Ketamine directly reduces channel mean open time by physically occluding the pore.
It decreases the frequency of channel opening through an allosteric mechanism.
Ketamine exhibits use-dependent blockade
Meaning it preferentially blocks channels that are already open and conducting ions. 
The drug can access its binding site by diffusing across the cell membrane, suggesting it reaches the channel from the intracellular side.
The primary mechanism at the NMDA receptor is straightforward: ketamine blocks the open channel, preventing the normal influx of calcium and sodium ions that would occur when glutamate and glycine activate the receptor.
It does not inhibit the efflux of potassium.
This blockade of calcium entry is particularly relevant to ketamine's effects on neuronal excitability and its potential neuroprotective properties.
As a racemic mixture, the S-ketamine enantiomer demonstrates approximately four times greater potency at NMDA receptors compared to the R-enantiomer.

Transition from Fluid Responsiveness to Fluid Intolerance
Heart is operating on the flat portion of the Frank-Starling curve, and additional fluids are more likely to accumulate in the lungs rather than improve cardiac output.
 Clinically, this overlap signals a high risk for pulmonary edema and impaired gas exchange, especially in patients with underlying lung injury or ARDS.

Endothelial Glycocalyx
Robust evidence for endothelial glycocalyx shedding with excess volume expansion. 
In critically ill patients, such as those with sepsis, the degree of glycocalyx shedding correlates with severity of illness and is independently associated with increased in-hospital mortality.[3-5]
After administration of a fluid bolus to a critically ill, unstable patient, only about 15–25% of the infused crystalloid volume remains in the intravascular space within 30 minutes. 
Majority of the fluid rapidly redistributes to the interstitial compartment due to increased capillary permeability, altered endothelial glycocalyx integrity, and the pathophysiology of critical illness, including sepsis and shock.
Although most crystalloid fluid redistributes to the interstitial space within hours, the transient increase in intravascular volume is sufficient to reverse shock physiology, improve cardiac output, and facilitate oxygen delivery to vital organs.
Most pronounced in the initial phase of resuscitation, when patients are profoundly volume depleted.
The key distinction is timing: crystalloid provides 50-60% plasma expansion during infusion due to incomplete distribution, but this rapidly falls to 15-25% within 30 minutes after the infusion ends

Normal Saline and Kidneys
Clinical trial data and observational studies provide further clarity on the timing and magnitude of renal effects associated with normal saline in septic shock. The initial bolus of normal saline (typically 30 mL/kg) is designed to rapidly restore intravascular volume and improve macro- and microvascular perfusion, which is essential for reversing hypoperfusion and preventing early organ injury. During this phase, the risk of decreased renal perfusion and acute kidney injury is minimal and outweighed by the benefits of prompt resuscitation.
Fluid administration continued beyond the initial resuscitation, especially with repeated or high-volume normal saline infusions, the supraphysiologic chloride load can induce hyperchloremic acidosis, stimulate tubuloglomerular feedback, and promote afferent arteriolar vasoconstriction. These mechanisms contribute to decreased renal perfusion and increased risk of acute kidney injury after the initial resuscitation phase, particularly when normal saline is used for ongoing fluid therapy rather than as a one-time bolus.
In patients with renal failure, normal saline is associated with a higher risk of hyperkalemia compared to lactated Ringer’s solution. This is primarily due to the development of hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis with normal saline, which shifts potassium extracellularly and impairs renal potassium excretion, as demonstrated in both adult and pediatric studies.[1-5] Despite containing potassium (typically 4 mEq/L), lactated Ringer’s does not independently increase the risk of hyperkalemia in patients with reduced kidney function, and may actually be associated with fewer cases of severe hyperkalemia compared to normal saline

Lactated Ringers and Potassium
The potassium content of lactated Ringer’s is lower than that of plasma, and the risk of hyperkalemia is not increased with standard resuscitation volumes, even in patients with advanced kidney disease.[5][9] In contrast, normal saline can cause a clinically significant rise in serum potassium, especially with large-volume administration, as shown in renal transplant and critical care cohorts.

Fluids in DKA
Balanced crystalloids are preferred over normal saline for fluid resuscitation in unstable patients with diabetic ketoacidosis due to faster DKA resolution and fewer metabolic complications.
Multiple recent randomized trials and meta-analyses demonstrate that balanced crystalloids (such as lactated Ringer’s or Plasma-Lyte) result in more rapid resolution of DKA and less hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis compared to normal saline, with similar or improved renal outcomes and no increase in adverse events.
Balanced crystalloids are associated with lower post-resuscitation chloride and sodium levels, higher bicarbonate levels, and a shorter duration of insulin infusion or DKA protocol, while normal saline is linked to more frequent hyperchloremia and slower DKA resolution.
Although guidelines have traditionally recommended normal saline, accumulating evidence supports the use of balanced crystalloids as the preferred initial resuscitation fluid in unstable DKA patients, and there are no significant safety barriers to their use.

Hematocrit / Blood Loss
Hematocrit typically decreases within minutes to hours after internal blood loss, as rapid fluid shifts from the interstitial space into the vasculature dilute the remaining red blood cells. 
The traditional teaching was that hematocrit does not immediately reflect acute blood loss due to delayed equilibration, but recent evidence shows that fluid shifts occur rapidly, and hematocrit can drop early, especially in severe hemorrhage or when resuscitation fluids are administered

The most reliable laboratory markers that suggest dehydration are elevated plasma osmolality (pOsm), increased blood urea nitrogen (BUN)/creatinine ratio, and, in some contexts, elevated serum sodium.

Monocyte Distribution Width (MDW) is a hematology parameter that measures the variability in size (volume) of circulating monocytes
It is calculated automatically as part of a complete blood count with differential.
Reflects morphological changes that occur when monocytes become activated during sepsis. 
Normal value: < 20
MDW ≥ 20 for sepsis detection

Specificity of 54-83% for sepsis

Proteinuria and Sepsis
Proteinuria in the context of sepsis is an indicator of acute kidney injury (AKI) and reflects increased glomerular permeability due to systemic inflammation and endothelial dysfunction. 
Presence of proteinuria, especially microalbuminuria, is associated with early impairment of the glomerular filtration barrier, even before overt changes in serum creatinine or urine output are detected. 
Driven by the inflammatory response and capillary leak characteristic of sepsis, leading to leakage of proteins such as albumin into the urine

Inflammatory Markers
C-reactive protein (CRP), procalcitonin (PCT), and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) are fundamentally different tools used in the diagnosis and management of infection and sepsis.

C-Reactive Protein (CRP)
<10 mg/L: Generally considered normal or minimal inflammation
>100 mg/L: 80-85% likelihood of bacterial infection
Acute-phase protein synthesized by the liver in response to inflammation, rising within 12–24 hours and peaking at 48 hours after an insult. 
Sensitive but not specific for bacterial infection. 
Can be elevated in any inflammatory condition, including viral infections and non-infectious causes

Procalcitonin
Normal procalcitonin levels are very low, typically less than 0.05-0.1 ng/mL (or μg/L) in healthy individuals. In healthy states, procalcitonin is essentially undetectable, with circulating levels generally below 0.05 ng/mL.
The interpretation of procalcitonin levels follows a tiered approach based on clinical context:
≤0.1 ng/mL: Effectively rules out bacteremia and serious bacterial infection
0.1-0.25 ng/mL: Low levels where serious bacterial infection is unlikely; may indicate early or localized infection
0.25-0.5 ng/mL: Moderate elevation suggesting possible bacterial infection
0.5-1.0 ng/mL: Indicative of localized bacterial infection
1.0-5.0 ng/mL: Suggestive of bacteremia or systemic infection
>5.0 ng/mL: Strongly indicative of severe sepsis
Procalcitonin rises rapidly within 4-6 hours in response to severe bacterial infection, with levels correlating to infection severity.
The median cutoff used in critically ill patients for distinguishing sepsis from other inflammatory conditions is approximately 1.1 ng/mL (IQR 0.5-2.0).
 Different clinical settings may use different thresholds
ICU settings typically use 0.5 μg/L while emergency department and primary care settings often use 0.25 μg/L for guiding antibiotic decisions.
Precursor of calcitonin, produced in response to bacterial infection, rising within 4 hours and peaking at 6–8 hours. 
More specific for bacterial infections than CRP, with higher diagnostic accuracy for sepsis and better discrimination between bacterial and viral etiologies. 
PCT levels correlate with infection severity and decrease rapidly after effective antibiotic therapy.
The magnitude of procalcitonin (PCT) increase correlates with the severity of sepsis, and higher PCT values are more commonly seen in infections caused by gram-negative bacteria compared to gram-positive bacteria, but PCT alone cannot reliably distinguish the causative organism
PCT levels are significantly higher in gram-negative bacteremia than in gram-positive bacteremia or candidemia
Median PCT values for gram-negative infections often exceed 2–10 ng/mL
Gram-positive infections: lower values (often <1 ng/mL)
The highest PCT levels are typically seen in infections with Enterobacteriaceae (e.g., E. coli, Klebsiella) and in urogenital or abdominal foci

Used to amplify and detect specific DNA or RNA sequences from pathogens (bacteria, viruses, fungi) in clinical samples.

Does not measure host response or inflammation.

ABG vs VBG
Agreement: pH, pCO2, HCO3, Lactate
pH: Venous pH is typically 0.03–0.05 units lower than arterial pH. This difference is consistent across studies and is considered clinically acceptable for initial acid-base assessment in most ICU and ED patients.
pCO₂: Venous pCO₂ is usually 4–6 mm Hg higher than arterial pCO₂. While the correlation is strong, the limits of agreement are wider, so VBG pCO₂ can be used to screen for hypercapnia but is less reliable for precise quantification.

PEEP Challenges in ARDS
Personal considerations:
The most suitable PEEP adjusted for compliance or driving pressure changes with the current volume. Because of this, starting PEEP in VCV with constant Vt and then venting in PCV allowing variations of Vt may not be the best thing to do
It doesn't seem to make sense to adjust the PEEP 2 сmH20 above the one with better compliance or lower driving pressure. This can promote hyperinflation and the risk of lung injury, especially when ventilated with high PEEP.
The Holy Grail search for the best method to hold PEEP in ARDS continues.
Improving P/F ratio does not always indicate clinical improvement of the patient
You can improve P/F at the cost of potentially injurious ventilation.
In most cases, ARDS patients do not die of hypoxemia, they die of shock, which can be amplified by biotrauma provided by a lung injured by an aggressive VM. 
Pulmonary heterogeneity: Especially in cases like ARDS, the lung becomes a mosaic of collapsed, hyperinflated, and normal regions — the so-called “baby lungs.” Adjusting a single PEEP that fits all of these zones is paradoxical to say the least. There will always be loss at some end.

Crystalloid Redistribution
The more critically ill the patient is, the more rapidly and extensively a crystalloid fluid bolus will redistribute into the interstitial space primarily due to increased vascular permeability and altered fluid kinetics in these states.
Not all forms of shock share this pathophysiology:
Hemorrhagic shock without significant inflammatory activation, the endothelial barrier may remain relatively intact, so redistribution of crystalloids into the interstitial space is less pronounced and slower compared to septic shock.
Cardiogenic shock, the primary issue is pump failure rather than capillary leak, so while fluid overload can occur, the redistribution kinetics are not as dramatically altered as in sepsis or severe inflammation

Complete vs. Incomplete SCI
Complete: absence of any sensory or motor function in the sacral segments of S4-S5.
Incomplete: “sacral sparing”; preservation of any sensory or motor function in these lowest sacral segments.
Importance: reflects whether any neural signals can travel through the injury site to the most caudal portion of the spinal cord. Even if a patient has some movement or sensation below their neurological level of injury, the injury is still classified as complete if there is no function preserved in or around the anus.

Coagulopathy in trauma patients begins to develop at core body temperatures below 35°C (95ºF), with critical impairment occurring below 34°C (93.2ºF).

Paradoxical hemodynamic decline following pericardiocentesis 
Occurs in patients who initially improve after drainage
Develop hypotension, pulmonary edema, reduce LV function 2-3 hours after procedure.
3 Mechanisms:
Ischemic: Prolonged tamponade causes myocardial ischemia due to reduced coronary perfusion. Ischemic myocardium may not recover function, leading to transient cardiogenic shock.
Hemodynamic: Decompression causes sudden change in ventricular loading. LV has adapted to compressed state with reduced preload. Abrupt change now causes LV to face increased venous return and wall stress, leads to LV dysfunction. 
Autonomic: Sudden relief may trigger autonomic dysregulation
Risk factors:
Large volume drainage
Rapid, uncontrolled drainage - controlled pericardial drainage preferred approach. Drain only enough to restore perfusion via intermittent cycles to maintain SBP ~ 90.

Hyponatremia in Acute Liver Failure
Initiated by portal hypertension and splanchnic vasodilation.
Increased intrahepatic resistance from liver injury causes portal hypertension, which activates vasodilators (particularly nitric oxide) in the splanchnic circulation.
Progressive vasodilation reduces effective arterial blood volume despite total body volume expansion with ascites.
Reduced effective arterial blood volume triggers compensatory neurohormonal activation, including RAAS, SNS, and most critically, nonosmotic ADH secretion.
ADH release occurs despite hypo-osmolality because the hemodynamic stimulus (~15% reduction in effective blood volume) overrides normal osmotic regulation.
Inappropriate ADH secretion promotes avid water retention in the renal collecting ducts while sodium continues to be retained, but water retention exceeds sodium retention. 


Acute Liver Failure / Ammonia / Cerebral Edema
Sustained arterial ammonia levels of 150 - 200 µmol/L (255-340 µg/dL) carry the greatest risk of intracranial hypertension, with this threshold presenting a critical inflection point where ammonia-induced cerebral edema becomes clinically significant.

Liver Failure / Vasopressin Deficiency
ALF does not cause a deficiency of vasopressin (ADH)
Typically causes inappropriate excess vasopressin secretion through nonosmotic stimulation, which is the primary mechanism underlying hyponatremia in liver failure.
Paradox: vasopressin levels are elevated in the context of hyponatremia, patients with end-stage liver disease can have relative vasopressin deficiency in the context of their hemodynamic state. 
Contradiction reflects dual role of vasopressin:
As antidiuretic regulating water balance
As vasoconstrictor supporting BP
In liver failure:
Vasopressin levels may be inappropriately elevated relative to the plasma.
Yet inappropriately low relative to the degree of vasodilation and hypotension (contributing to vasodilatory shock)

Vasopressin is inappropriately elevated in ALF, not deficient, when considering plasma osmolality
Nonosmotic stimulation drives excess ADH release due to reduced effective arterial blood volume from splanchnic vasodilation
This causes dilutional hyponatremia (water retention exceeding sodium retention)
The Paradox: Relative Deficiency in Shock
Despite elevated ADH for osmolality, levels may be relatively low for the degree of hypotension
Patients with vasopressin ≤20 pg/mL are more likely hypotensive and respond briskly to exogenous vasopressin
This explains why vasopressin works as a vasopressor despite "excess" secretion

Target hemodynamics:
MAP >75 mmHg and cerebral perfusion pressure 60-80 mmHg
Bottom Line
Vasopressin is elevated relative to osmolality (causing hyponatremia) but deficient relative to hemodynamic needs (contributing to shock)—making exogenous vasopressin useful for refractory hypotension but not for correcting hyponatremia.

CO typically decreases in the early phase of septic shock (0-24 hours) in the absence of adequate fluid resuscitation
Can remain normal or elevated after volume resuscitation due to compensatory mechanisms

With vigorous fluid resuscitation, most patients develop a hyperdynamic state with normal or increased cardiac output despite underlying myocardial dysfunction.
Advanced and Late Septic Shock
When CO progressively declines despite resuscitation
Involves varying degrees of venous and myocardial contractile failure
Progressive acidemia
Marked sympathetic activation.
In absence of adequate fluid resuscitation, patients develop decreased CO with cold, cammy peripheries. 
Late sepsis is characterized by profound acidemia, vascular hyporesponsiveness, multiple organ failure, and death. 
Hyperdynamic state with preserved or elevated CO typically persists “until death or recovery” in adequately resuscitated patients.
Meaning, overt CO reduction is “decidedly uncommon”, even in the preterminal phase” when resuscitation is maintained. 

Myocardial Dysfunction in Septic Shock
Not from myocardial ischemia or cell death.
Inflammatory mediators play central role
Cytokines (TNF-a, IL-1B) act as circulating myocardial depressant factors. LPS bind to Toll-like receptors on cardiomyocytes.
NO Overproduction: impairs contractility. 
Mitochondrial Dysfunction: downregulation of oxidative phosphorylation enzymes decreases ATP production.
Impaired Calcium Handling and Electromechanical Coupling: B-adrenergic receptor downregulation, depressed postreceptor signaling, impaired calcium liberation for sarcoplasmic reticulum, altered cardiomyocyte sodium currents that affect depolarization.
Myocardial edema: through bradykinin receptor B1-mediated increases in microvascular permeability.

For patients with documented allergic reactions to morphine, codeine, hydromorphone, or other phenanthrene opioids, fentanyl represents a safe alternative with negligible risk of cross-reactivity.

title: Բնակչությունը։ Հարավարևմտյան Ասիայում բնակվում է 4...
original_text:
Բնակչությունը։ Հարավարևմտյան Ասիայում բնակվում է 412 մլն մարդ (2021թ.): Տարածաշրջանի բնակչության բաժինը երկրագնդի ամբողջ բնակչության մեջ կազմում է 5,2%,
ինչը գրեթե հավասար է երկրագնդի բնակելի տարածքի մեջ այդ տարածաշրջանի տեսակարար կշռին
(5,3%): (Նկ. 10) Դա նշանակում է, որ Հարավարևմտյան
Ասիան ամբողջությամբ երկրագնդի բնակչության հետ
համաչափ է բնակեցված:
Տարածաշրջանի 19 երկրները բնակչության թվով
շատ մեծ չափով են տարբերվում միմյանցից: Այսպես
օրինակ՝ ամենամեծաթիվ բնակչություն ունեցող
երկրներից Թուրքիայի բնակչությունը՝ 87 մլն մարդ,
տարածաշրջանի ամենափոքրաթիվ բնակչություն
ունեցող երկրին՝ Բահրեյնին (1,5 մլն) գերազանցում է
57 անգամ, իսկ Իրանի բնակչության թիվը նույն 
երկրին գերազանցում է 58 անգամ: 31-ից մինչև 45 մլն բնակիչ ունեցող, միջին մեծության
երկրներ են Եմենը, Սաուդյան Արաբիան, Աֆղանստանը և Իրաքը:
Հարավարևմտյան Ասիայի բնակչության բնական աճը տարեկան 1,5% է, ինչը մոտ
կրկնակի բարձր է ամբողջ աշխարհի համապատասխան ցուցանիշից (0,8%): Այդպիսի
գերազանցությունը բացատրվում է այն հանգամանքով, որ տարածաշրջանի երկրների
մեծ մասում բնակվում են գերազանցապես մահմեդական կրոն դավանող ժողովուրդներ:
Հայտնի է, որ նրանց բնական աճը զգալիորեն բարձր է քրիստոնյաների աճից:
Տարածաշրջանի միջին ցուցանիշից բարձր տեմպով է աճում Սիրիայի, Աֆղանստանի,
Իրաքի բնակչության թիվը, իսկ ամենաբարձր աճի տեմպ է գրանցվում նավթարդյունաբերական երկրներում: Այդ երկրների նավթարդյունաբերության ճյուղի ձեռնարկություններում, ինչպես նաև սպասարկման ծառայությունների ոլորտում աշխատելու համար
այլ երկրներից (գլխավորապես արաբական և Հնդկական օվկիանոսի ավազանի
երկրներից) ներգաղթում են երիտասարդ տղամարդիկ, ինչի հետևանքով տղամարդկանց
թիվը մեծ չափով գերազանցում է կանանց թվին:
Հարավարևմտյան Ասիան համաշխարհային կրոններից երկուսի՝ քրիստոնեության և
մահմեդականության օրրանն է:
Սաուդյան Արաբիայի Մեքքա քաղաքն
աշխարհի մահմեդականների սրբատեղին է:
Ամեն տարի այստեղ ուխտագնացության են
գալիս միլիոնավոր մահմեդականներ: Սրբավայր է նաև Մեդինա քաղաքը, որտեղ հանգչում
է Մուհամմեդ մարգարեի աճյունը:
Այս տարածաշրջանում է ծագել նաև
քրիստոնեական կրոնը: Ըստ ավանդույթի՝
Քրիստոսը ծնվել է Բեթղեհեմում, խաչվել է
Գողգոթայում, թաղվել է Երուսաղեմում: Թաղման
վայրին մոտ գործում է հայկական հին եկեղեցի:
Երուսաղեմը նաև հրեաների դավանանքի՝
հուդայականության օրրանն է:
Հարավարևմտյան Ասիայի բնակչությունը
բնութագրվում է ազգային կազմի խայտաբղետությամբ: Բնակչության ընդհանուր քանակում մեծ են արաբների, Իրանի պարսիկների, թուրքերի, աֆղանների, քրդերի բաժինները:
Լեզվական կազմի տեսանկյունից ևս Հարավարևմտյան Ասիայի բնակչությունը
միատարր չէ: Սեմական խմբի լեզուներով խոսող ժողովուրդներից են արաբներն ու
Իսրայելի հրեաները*
: Իրանական խմբի լեզուներով խոսում են պարսիկները, աֆղանները,
քրդերը, թուրքական խմբի լեզուներով՝ թուրքերն ու ադրբեջանցիները:
Տարածաշրջանի բնակչությունը գլխավորապես կենտրոնացած է բնակլիմայական
համեմատաբար նպաստավոր պայմաններ ունեցող, ոչ ընդարձակ շրջաններում,
առավելապես միջլեռնային գոգավորություններում, գետահովիտներում, օազիսներում և
ծովափնյա գոտում, որտեղ կլիման մեղմ է ու խոնավ:
Քաղաքային բնակչության բաժինն արագորեն աճել է վերջին տասնամյակներում՝
նավթարդյունաբերության բուռն զարգացման պատճառով: Ձևավորվել են ժամանակակից
երկնաքերներով կառուցապատված քաղաքներ: Քաղաքներում բնակչության թվի աճի
մյուս պատճառը տարածաշրջանի սակավահողությունն է, որի պայմաններում հողազուրկ
գյուղացիները գաղթում են տարածաշրջանի քաղաքներ՝ աշխատանք գտնելու: Այդ բոլորի
հետևանքով տարածաշրջանի քաղաքային բնակչության բաժինը (քաղաքայնացման
մակարդակը)՝ 72%, շատ բարձր է Ասիայի մյուս տարածաշրջանների, ինչպես նաև ամբողջ
աշխարհի միջին ցուցանիշից (մոտ 56%):
Տարածաշրջանում կան մի շարք խոշոր քաղաքներ՝ Ստամբուլը, Թեհրանը, Անկարան,
Հալեպը, Մաշհադը, Ամմանը:
Տնտեսությունը: Հարավարևմտյան Ասիա տարածաշրջանի տնտեսության բուռն
զարգացումը սկսվել է քաղաքական անկախություն ձեռք բերելուց հետո, երկրների մեծ
մասում նավթի և բնական գազի հսկայական պաշարների շահագործման շնորհիվ, իսկ
այն երկրները, որոնք չունեն նավթի և բնական գազի մեծ պաշարներ, օրինակ՝ Կիպրոսը,
Լիբանանը, Իսրայելը, Թուրքիան, զարգացրել են բազմաճյուղ արդյունաբերություն՝ օգտագործելով իրենց այլ հանքային հարստությունները և գյուղատնտեսական արտադրանքը:
Պատմական համեմատաբար կարճ ժամանակամիջոցում տարածաշրջանը հասավ
աշխարհի մասշտաբով միջին մակարդակի: Մեկ մարդու հաշվով ՀՆԱ-ն շատ բարձր,
նույնիսկ ռեկորդային մակարդակի է հասել Քաթարում, Քուվեյթում, ՄԱԱ-ում*
, Սաուդյան
Արաբիայում: Համեմատության համար ընդգծենք, որ այդ ցուցանիշը 5–13 անգամ բարձր է
ՀՀ կենսամակարդակի ցուցանիշից։
Տարածաշրջանն աշխարհում աչքի է ընկնում նաև բնական գազի հանույթի ծավալով:
Մասնավորապես՝ Իրանն աշխարհում զբաղեցնում է երրորդ, Քաթարը՝ վեցերորդ տեղը:
Այլ հանքանյութերից առավել շատ արդյունահանում են ֆոսֆորիտ (Հորդանան, Իրաք),
կալիումական աղ (Հորդանան, Իսրայել), քրոմիտ (Թուրքիա, Իրան), բնական ծծումբ
(Իրաք):
Մշակող արդյունաբերական ճյուղերից զարգացած է սև մետաղաձուլությունը՝
Թուրքիայում, Իրանում, ինչպես նաև թեթև, առանձնապես տեքստիլ արդյունաբերությունը:
Դրանում առավել մեծ է բամբակե տեքստիլ արտադրանքի բաժինը:
Վերջին տարիներին համեմատաբար արագորեն զարգանում է նաև մեքենաշինությունը, հատկապես ավտոմոբիլաշինությունը (Թուրքիա, Իրան): Իրանում և Թուրքիայում
սկսել են արտադրել անօդաչու թռչող սարքեր, հրթիռներ, ռազմական արտադրատեսակներ:
Հարավարևմտյան Ասիան աշխարհում առաջատար դիրք է զբաղեցնում բուսական
ծագման մթերքների արտադրության գծով: Օրինակ՝ Իրաքն արտադրում է փյունիկյան
արմավի համաշխարհային համախառն բերքի շուրջ 4/5-ը:
Թուրքիան հայտնի է որպես չորացրած մրգերի խոշոր արտադրող: Թուրքիան նաև
աշխարհում կաղինի (պնդուկ) խոշորագույն արտահանողն է: Տարածաշրջանն աշխարհում առաջատարներից է նաև բամբակի, խաղողի, նուշի, ծխախոտի բերքի
մեծությամբ:
Անասնապահությունը տարածաշրջանի համեմատաբար թույլ զարգացած ճյուղն է:
Այն, ի տարբերություն Եվրոպայի և Հյուսիսային Ամերիկայի, ունի էքստենսիվ բնույթ՝ պայմանավորված բնակլիմայական ոչ շատ բարենպաստ պայմաններով:
Հարավարևմտյան Ասիան աշխարհի առաջատար տարածաշրջաններից է մանր
եղջերավոր կենդանիների գլխաքանակով: Գլխավոր ճյուղը ոչխարապահությունն է: Այս
տարածաշրջանում զարգացած ենթաճյուղ է նաև այծերի բուծումը: Տեղական՝ անգորական
ցեղի երկարամազ բուրդ տվող այծերի բուծմամբ հայտնի է Թուրքիան, իսկ քերմանի
այծերի բուծմամբ՝ Իրանը: Անապատային տարածքներում շարունակում են զբաղվել
ուղտաբուծությամբ:
Որպես առավելապես մահմեդականներով բնակեցված տարածաշրջան՝ Հարավարևմտյան Ասիայի անասնապահության թերազարգացած օղակը խոզաբուծությունն է:
Տարածաշրջանը բանուկ միջազգային մայրուղիների խաչմերուկում զբաղեցնում է
կենտրոնական դիրք: Այս տարածաշրջանից՝ Պարսից ծոցից է սկիզբ առնում նավթի
ծովային փոխադրման ամենամեծ հոսքը դեպի աշխարհի տարբեր տարածաշրջանների
սպառողական կենտրոններ:
Նավթի առաքման խոշոր նավահանգիստներից եբ Ռաս Տանուրան (Սաուդյան
Արաբիա), Բանդար է Խոմեյնին (Իրան): Ծովային փոխադրումների բանուկ ուղիներն
անցնում են Բաբելմանդեբ, Բոսֆոր, Դարդանել նեղուցներով:
Տարածաշրջանում բավականին զարգացած է խողովակաշարային տրանսպորտը:
Դրանով Հարավարևմտյան Ասիայի տարբեր մասերից նավթը հասցվում է ծովափնյա
նավահանգիստներ: Համեմատաբար խոշոր են Քիրքուկ (Իրաք)–Տրիպոլի (Լիբանան),
Քիրքուկ–Հայֆա (Իսրայել), Աբքայք (Սաուդյան Արաբիա)–Հայֆա նավթամուղները:
Տարածաշրջանում կան միջազգային նշանակության մի շարք խոշոր օդանավակայաններ: Դրանցից են Ստամբուլի, Դուբայի, Դոհայի օդանավակայանները:
Տարածաշրջանում երկաթուղային տրանսպորտը համեմատաբար զարգացած է մեծ
տարածք ունեցող երկրներում՝ Իրանում և Թուրքիայում: Արաբական թերակղզու երկրներում, որտեղ բացակայում է երկաթուղին, ներքին փոխադրումներ իրականացնող
տրանսպորտային գլխավոր տեսակը ավտոմոբիլայինն է:
1. Բնութագրե՛ք Հարավարևմտյան Ասիայի բնակչության աճը։
2. Բնութագրե՛ք տարածաշրջանի բնակչության կրոնական և լեզվական կազմը:
3. Ո՞ր (ինչպիսի՞) տարածքներում է կենտրոնացած տարածաշրջանի բնակչությունը:
4. Բնորոշե՛ք տարածաշրջանի քաղաքայնացման գործընթացի գլխավոր գործոնները:
5. Ըստ տնտեսական զարգացման (մասնագիտացման) ուղղության՝ երկրների ինչպիսի՞
խմբեր կան տարածաշրջանում:
6. Բնութագրե՛ք, տնտեսության առաջատար ճյուղերից բացի, արդյունաբերության և
գյուղատնտեսության զարգացած այլ ճյուղերը։
7. Քննարկե՛ք և ներկայացրե՛ք ձեր դիրքորոշումը համաշխարհային կրոնների՝ քրիստոնեության և մահմեդականության վերաբերյալ։ Հիմնավորե՛ք ձեր տեսակետը

For example, $7 \\div 3$ is 2 with a remainder of 1.","key_takeaway":"The result of a modulus operation is always smaller than the divisor."},{"title":"Applications in Programming","summary":"Modulus is widely used to determine if a number is even or odd (checking $x \\% 2$), to constrain values within a range (circular arrays), and in cryptography.","key_takeaway":"If $x \\% n = 0$, then $x$ is a multiple of $n$."},{"title":"Clock Arithmetic","summary":"The most common real-world example of modulus is the 12-hour clock. If it is 10:00 and 4 hours pass, the time is 2:00. Mathematically, $(10 + 4) \\pmod{12} = 2$.","key_takeaway":"Modulus allows for cyclical or wrap-around logic."}],"three_bullet_summary":["The modulus operator returns the remainder of a division between two numbers rather than the quotient.","It is commonly represented by the percent symbol `%` in programming or the keyword 'mod' in mathematics.","The operation $a \\pmod{n}$ results in the remainder after $a$ is divided by $n$."]}

WikipediaThe Free Encyclopedia Search Wikipedia Search Donate Create account Log in Contents hide (Top) Characteristics History Alternative definitions Implementations Hosting Trust and security Communities Legal environment Conferences See also Notes References Further reading External links Wiki Article Talk Read View source View history Tools Appearance hide Text Small Standard Large Width Standard Wide Color (beta) Automatic Light Dark Page extended-confirmed-protected Listen to this article From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia This article is about the concept. For its use as a nickname, see Wikipedia. For other uses, see Wiki (disambiguation). Not to be confused with Wiiki or WIKY. refer to caption Editing display showing MediaWiki markup language A photo of the MediaWiki homepage, a wiki software A wiki (/ˈwɪki/ ⓘ WICK-ee) is a form of hypertext publication on the internet which is collaboratively edited and managed by its audience directly through a web browser. A typical wiki contains multiple pages that can either be edited by the public or limited to use within an organization for maintaining its internal knowledge base. Its name derives from the first user-editable website called WikiWikiWeb – wiki (pronounced [wiki][note 1]) is a Hawaiian word meaning 'quick'.[1][2][3][4] Wikis are powered by wiki software, also known as wiki engines. Being a form of content management system, these differ from other web-based systems such as blog software or static site generators in that the content is created without any defined owner or leader. Wikis have little inherent structure, allowing one to emerge according to the needs of the users.[5] Wiki engines usually allow content to be written using a lightweight markup language and sometimes edited with the help of a rich-text editor.[6] There are dozens of different wiki engines in use, both standalone and part of other software, such as bug tracking systems. Some wiki engines are free and open-source, whereas others are proprietary. Some permit control over different functions (levels of access); for example, editing rights may permit changing, adding, or removing material. Others may permit access without enforcing access control. Further rules may be imposed to organize content. In addition to hosting user-authored content, wikis allow those users to interact, hold discussions, and collaborate.[7] There are hundreds of thousands of wikis in use, both public and private, including wikis functioning as knowledge management resources, note-taking tools, community websites, and intranets. Ward Cunningham, the developer of the first wiki software, WikiWikiWeb, originally described wiki as "the simplest online database that could possibly work".[8] The online encyclopedia project Wikipedia is the most popular wiki-based website, as well being one of the internet's most popular websites, having been ranked consistently as such since at least 2007.[9] Wikipedia is not a single wiki but rather a collection of hundreds of wikis, with each one pertaining to a specific language, making it the largest reference work of all time.[10] The English-language Wikipedia has the largest collection of articles, standing at 7,121,553 as of January 2026.[11] Characteristics Ward Cunningham Ward Cunningham In their 2001 book The Wiki Way: Quick Collaboration on the Web, Ward Cunningham and co-author Bo Leuf described the essence of the wiki concept:[12][13] "A wiki invites all users—not just experts—to edit any page or to create new pages within the wiki website, using only a standard 'plain-vanilla' Web browser without any extra add-ons." "Wiki promotes meaningful topic associations between different pages by making page link creation intuitively easy and showing whether an intended target page exists or not." "A wiki is not a carefully crafted site created by experts and professional writers and designed for casual visitors. Instead, it seeks to involve the typical visitor/user in an ongoing process of creation and collaboration that constantly changes the website landscape." Editing "Wikitext" redirects here. For the Wikipedia help page, see Help:Wikitext. Source editing Some wikis will present users with an edit button or link directly on the page being viewed. This will open an interface for writing, formatting, and structuring page content. The interface may be a source editor, which is text-based and employs a lightweight markup language (also known as wikitext, wiki markup, or wikicode), or a visual editor. For example, in a source editor, starting lines of text with asterisks could create a bulleted list. The syntax and features of wiki markup languages for denoting style and structure can vary greatly among implementations. Some allow the use of HTMLTooltip Hypertext Markup Language and CSSTooltip Cascading Style Sheets,[14] while others prevent the use of these to foster uniformity in appearance. Example of syntax A short section of the 1865 novel Alice's Adventures in Wonderland rendered in wiki markup: Wiki markup Equivalent in HTML Output shown to readers "Take some more [[tea]]," the March Hare said to Alice, very earnestly. "I've had '''nothing''' yet," Alice replied in an offended tone, "so I can't take more." "You mean you can't take ''less''," said the Hatter. "It's very easy to take ''more'' than nothing."

"Take some more tea," the March Hare said to Alice, very earnestly.

"I've had nothing yet," Alice replied in an offended tone, "so I can't take more."

"You mean you can't take less," said the Hatter. "It's very easy to take more than nothing." "Take some more tea," the March Hare said to Alice, very earnestly. "I've had nothing yet," Alice replied in an offended tone, "so I can't take more." "You mean you can't take less," said the Hatter. "It's very easy to take more than nothing." Visual editing While wiki engines have traditionally offered source editing to users, in recent years some implementations have added a rich text editing mode. This is usually implemented, using JavaScript, as an interface which translates formatting instructions chosen from a toolbar into the corresponding wiki markup or HTML. This is generated and submitted to the server transparently, shielding users from the technical detail of markup editing and making it easier for them to change the content of pages. An example of such an interface is the VisualEditor in MediaWiki, the wiki engine used by Wikipedia. WYSIWYG editors may not provide all the features available in wiki markup, and some users prefer not to use them, so a source editor will often be available simultaneously. Version history Some wiki implementations keep a record of changes made to wiki pages, and may store every version of the page permanently. This allows authors to revert a page to an older version to rectify a mistake, or counteract a malicious or inappropriate edit to its content.[15] These stores are typically presented for each page in a list, called a "log" or "edit history", available from the page via a link in the interface. The list displays metadata for each revision to the page, such as the time and date of when it was stored, and the name of the person who created it, alongside a link to view that specific revision. A diff (short for "difference") feature may be available, which highlights the changes between any two revisions. Edit summaries "Edit summary" redirects here. For the Wikipedia help page, see Help:Edit summary. The edit history view in many wiki implementations will include edit summaries written by users when submitting changes to a page. Similar to the function of a log message in a revision control system, an edit summary is a short piece of text which summarizes and perhaps explains the change, for example "Corrected grammar" or "Fixed table formatting to not extend past page width". It is not inserted into the article's main text. Navigation Traditionally, wikis offer free navigation between their pages via hypertext links in page text, rather than requiring users to follow a formal or structured navigation scheme. Users may also create indexes or table of contents pages, hierarchical categorization via a taxonomy, or other forms of ad hoc content organization. Wiki implementations can provide one or more ways to categorize or tag pages to support the maintenance of such index pages, such as a backlink feature which displays all pages that link to a given page. Adding categories or tags to a page makes it easier for other users to find it. Most wikis allow the titles of pages to be searched amongst, and some offer full text search of all stored content. Navigation between wikis "WikiNode" redirects here. For the app for the Apple iPad, see WikiNodes. Visualization of the collaborative work in the German wiki project Mathe für Nicht-Freaks Some wiki communities have established navigational networks between each other using a system called WikiNodes. A WikiNode is a page on a wiki which describes and links to other, related wikis. Some wikis operate a structure of neighbors and delegates, wherein a neighbor wiki is one which discusses similar content or is otherwise of interest, and a delegate wiki is one which has agreed to have certain content delegated to it.[16] WikiNode networks act as webrings which may be navigated from one node to another to find a wiki which addresses a specific subject. Linking to and naming pages "wikilink" redirects here. For the Wikipedia linking how-to guide, see Help:Link. The syntax used to create internal hyperlinks varies between wiki implementations. Beginning with the WikiWikiWeb in 1995, most wikis used camel case to name pages,[17] which is when words in a phrase are capitalized and the spaces between them removed. In this system, the phrase "camel case" would be rendered as "CamelCase". In early wiki engines, when a page was displayed, any instance of a camel case phrase would be transformed into a link to another page named with the same phrase. While this system made it easy to link to pages, it had the downside of requiring pages to be named in a form deviating from standard spelling, and titles of a single word required abnormally capitalizing one of the letters (e.g. "WiKi" instead of "Wiki"). Some wiki implementations attempt to improve the display of camel case page titles and links by reinserting spaces and possibly also reverting to lower case, but this simplistic method is not able to correctly present titles of mixed capitalization. For example, "Kingdom of France" as a page title would be written as "KingdomOfFrance", and displayed as "Kingdom Of France". To avoid this problem, the syntax of wiki markup gained free links, wherein a term in natural language could be wrapped in special characters to turn it into a link without modifying it. The concept was given the name in its first implementation, in UseModWiki in February 2001.[18] In that implementation, link terms were wrapped in a double set of square brackets, for example [[Kingdom of France]]. This syntax was adopted by a number of later wiki engines. It is typically possible for users of a wiki to create links to pages that do not yet exist, as a way to invite the creation of those pages. Such links are usually differentiated visually in some fashion, such as being colored red instead of the default blue, which was the case in the original WikiWikiWeb, or by appearing as a question mark next to the linked words. History Main article: History of wikis Wiki Wiki Shuttle at Honolulu International Airport WikiWikiWeb was the first wiki.[19] Ward Cunningham started developing it in 1994, and installed it on the Internet domain c2.com on March 25, 1995. Cunningham gave it the name after remembering a Honolulu International Airport counter employee telling him to take the "Wiki Wiki Shuttle" bus that runs between the airport's terminals, later observing that "I chose wiki-wiki as an alliterative substitute for 'quick' and thereby avoided naming this stuff quick-web."[20][21] Cunningham's system was inspired by his having used Apple's hypertext software HyperCard, which allowed users to create interlinked "stacks" of virtual cards.[22] HyperCard, however, was single-user, and Cunningham was inspired to build upon the ideas of Vannevar Bush, the inventor of hypertext, by allowing users to "comment on and change one another's text".[6][23] Cunningham says his goals were to link together people's experiences to create a new literature to document programming patterns, and to harness people's natural desire to talk and tell stories with a technology that would feel comfortable to those not used to "authoring".[22] Wikipedia became the most famous wiki site[clarification needed], launched in January 2001 and entering the top ten most popular websites in 2007. In the early 2000s, wikis were increasingly adopted in enterprise as collaborative software. Common uses included project communication, intranets, and documentation, initially for technical users. Some companies use wikis as their collaborative software and as a replacement for static intranets, and some schools and universities use wikis to enhance group learning. On March 15, 2007, the word wiki was listed in the online Oxford English Dictionary.[24] Alternative definitions In the late 1990s and early 2000s, the word "wiki" was used to refer to both user-editable websites and the software that powers them, and the latter definition is still occasionally in use.[5] By 2014, Ward Cunningham's thinking on the nature of wikis had evolved, leading him to write[25] that the word "wiki" should not be used to refer to a single website, but rather to a mass of user-editable pages or sites so that a single website is not "a wiki" but "an instance of wiki". In this concept of wiki federation, in which the same content can be hosted and edited in more than one location in a manner similar to distributed version control, the idea of a single discrete "wiki" no longer made sense.[26] Implementations See also: List of wiki software The software which powers a wiki may be implemented as a series of scripts which operate an existing web server, a standalone application server that runs on one or more web servers, or in the case of personal wikis, run as a standalone application on a single computer. Some wikis use flat file databases to store page content, while others use a relational database,[27] as indexed database access is faster on large wikis, particularly for searching. Hosting See also: Comparison of wiki hosting services Wikis can also be created on wiki hosting services (also known as wiki farms), where the server-side software is implemented by the wiki farm owner, and may do so at no charge in exchange for advertisements being displayed on the wiki's pages. Some hosting services offer private, password-protected wikis requiring authentication to access. Free wiki farms generally contain advertising on every page. Trust and security Access control The four basic types of users who participate in wikis are readers, authors, wiki administrators and system administrators. System administrators are responsible for the installation and maintenance of the wiki engine and the container web server. Wiki administrators maintain content and, through having elevated privileges, are granted additional functions (including, for example, preventing edits to pages, deleting pages, changing users' access rights, or blocking them from editing).[28] Controlling changes "Recent changes" redirects here. For the Wikipedia help page, see Help:Recent changes. For the recent changes page itself, see Special:RecentChanges. History comparison reports highlight the changes between two revisions of a page. Wikis are generally designed with a soft security philosophy in which it is easy to correct mistakes or harmful changes, rather than attempting to prevent them from happening in the first place. This allows them to be very open while providing a means to verify the validity of recent additions to the body of pages. Most wikis offer a recent changes page which shows recent edits, or a list of edits made within a given time frame.[29] Some wikis can filter the list to remove edits flagged by users as "minor" and automated edits.[30] The version history feature allows harmful changes to be reverted quickly and easily.[15] Some wiki engines provide additional content control, allowing remote monitoring and management of a page or set of pages to maintain quality. A person willing to maintain pages will be alerted of modifications to them, allowing them to verify the validity of new editions quickly.[31] Such a feature is often called a watchlist. Some wikis also implement patrolled revisions, in which editors with the requisite credentials can mark edits as being legitimate. A flagged revisions system can prevent edits from going live until they have been reviewed.[32] Wikis may allow any person on the web to edit their content without having to register an account on the site first (anonymous editing), or require registration as a condition of participation.[33] On implementations where an administrator is able to restrict editing of a page or group of pages to a specific group of users, they may have the option to prevent anonymous editing while allowing it for registered users.[34] Trustworthiness and reliability of content Critics of publicly editable wikis argue that they could be easily tampered with by malicious individuals, or even by well-meaning but unskilled users who introduce errors into the content. Proponents maintain that these issues will be caught and rectified by a wiki's community of users.[6][19] High editorial standards in medicine and health sciences articles, in which users typically use peer-reviewed journals or university textbooks as sources, have led to the idea of expert-moderated wikis.[35] Wiki implementations retaining and allowing access to specific versions of articles has been useful to the scientific community, by allowing expert peer reviewers to provide links to trusted version of articles which they have analyzed.[36] Security "Edit war" redirects here; not to be confused with Edit conflict. For Wikipedia's policy on edit warring, see Wikipedia:Edit warring. Trolling and cybervandalism on wikis, where content is changed to something deliberately incorrect or a hoax, offensive material or nonsense is added, or content is maliciously removed, can be a major problem. On larger wiki sites it is possible for such changes to go unnoticed for a long period. In addition to using the approach of soft security for protecting themselves, larger wikis may employ sophisticated methods, such as bots that automatically identify and revert vandalism. For example, on Wikipedia, the bot ClueBot NG uses machine learning to identify likely harmful changes, and reverts these changes within minutes or even seconds.[37] Disagreements between users over the content or appearance of pages may cause edit wars, where competing users repetitively change a page back to a version that they favor. Some wiki software allows administrators to prevent pages from being editable until a decision has been made on what version of the page would be most appropriate.[7] Some wikis may be subject to external structures of governance which address the behavior of persons with access to the system, for example in academic contexts.[27] Harmful external links As most wikis allow the creation of hyperlinks to other sites and services, the addition of malicious hyperlinks, such as sites infected with malware, can also be a problem. For example, in 2006 a German Wikipedia article about the Blaster Worm was edited to include a hyperlink to a malicious website, and users of vulnerable Microsoft Windows systems who followed the link had their systems infected with the worm.[7] Some wiki engines offer a blacklist feature which prevents users from adding hyperlinks to specific sites that have been placed on the list by the wiki's administrators. Communities Applications The home page of the English Wikipedia, as of June 24, 2024 The English Wikipedia has the largest user base among wikis on the World Wide Web[38] and ranks in the top 10 among all Web sites in terms of traffic.[39] Other large wikis include the WikiWikiWeb, Memory Alpha, Wikivoyage, and previously Susning.nu, a Swedish-language knowledge base. Medical and health-related wiki examples include Ganfyd, an online collaborative medical reference that is edited by medical professionals and invited non-medical experts.[40] Many wiki communities are private, particularly within enterprises. They are often used as internal documentation for in-house systems and applications. Some companies use wikis to allow customers to help produce software documentation.[41] A study of corporate wiki users found that they could be divided into "synthesizers" and "adders" of content. Synthesizers' frequency of contribution was affected more by their impact on other wiki users, while adders' contribution frequency was affected more by being able to accomplish their immediate work.[42] From a study of thousands of wiki deployments, Jonathan Grudin concluded careful stakeholder analysis and education are crucial to successful wiki deployment.[43] In 2005, the Gartner Group, noting the increasing popularity of wikis, estimated that they would become mainstream collaboration tools in at least 50% of companies by 2009.[44][needs update] Wikis can be used for project management.[45][46][unreliable source] Wikis have also been used in the academic community for sharing and dissemination of information across institutional and international boundaries.[47] In those settings, they have been found useful for collaboration on grant writing, strategic planning, departmental documentation, and committee work.[48] In the mid-2000s, the increasing trend among industries toward collaboration placed a heavier impetus upon educators to make students proficient in collaborative work, inspiring even greater interest in wikis being used in the classroom.[7] Wikis have found some use within the legal profession and within the government. Examples include the Central Intelligence Agency's Intellipedia, designed to share and collect intelligence assessments, DKosopedia, which was used by the American Civil Liberties Union to assist with review of documents about the internment of detainees in Guantánamo Bay;[49] and the wiki of the United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit, used to post court rules and allow practitioners to comment and ask questions. The United States Patent and Trademark Office operates Peer-to-Patent, a wiki to allow the public to collaborate on finding prior art relevant to the examination of pending patent applications. Queens, New York has used a wiki to allow citizens to collaborate on the design and planning of a local park. Cornell Law School founded a wiki-based legal dictionary called Wex, whose growth has been hampered by restrictions on who can edit.[34] In academic contexts, wikis have also been used as project collaboration and research support systems.[50][51] City wikis A city wiki or local wiki is a wiki used as a knowledge base and social network for a specific geographical locale.[52][53][54] The term city wiki is sometimes also used for wikis that cover not just a city, but a small town or an entire region. Such a wiki contains information about specific instances of things, ideas, people and places. Such highly localized information might be appropriate for a wiki targeted at local viewers, and could include: Details of public establishments such as public houses, bars, accommodation or social centers Owner name, opening hours and statistics for a specific shop Statistical information about a specific road in a city Flavors of ice cream served at a local ice cream parlor A biography of a local mayor and other persons Growth factors A study of several hundred wikis in 2008 showed that a relatively high number of administrators for a given content size is likely to reduce growth;[55] access controls restricting editing to registered users tends to reduce growth; a lack of such access controls tends to fuel new user registration; and that a higher ratio of administrators to regular users has no significant effect on content or population growth.[56] Legal environment Joint authorship of articles, in which different users participate in correcting, editing, and compiling the finished product, can also cause editors to become tenants in common of the copyright, making it impossible to republish without permission of all co-owners, some of whose identities may be unknown due to pseudonymous or anonymous editing.[7] Some copyright issues can be alleviated through the use of an open content license. Version 2 of the GNU Free Documentation License includes a specific provision for wiki relicensing, and Creative Commons licenses are also popular. When no license is specified, an implied license to read and add content to a wiki may be deemed to exist on the grounds of business necessity and the inherent nature of a wiki. Wikis and their users can be held liable for certain activities that occur on the wiki. If a wiki owner displays indifference and forgoes controls (such as banning copyright infringers) that they could have exercised to stop copyright infringement, they may be deemed to have authorized infringement, especially if the wiki is primarily used to infringe copyrights or obtains a direct financial benefit, such as advertising revenue, from infringing activities.[7] In the United States, wikis may benefit from Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act, which protects sites that engage in "Good Samaritan" policing of harmful material, with no requirement on the quality or quantity of such self-policing.[57] It has also been argued that a wiki's enforcement of certain rules, such as anti-bias, verifiability, reliable sourcing, and no-original-research policies, could pose legal risks.[58] When defamation occurs on a wiki, theoretically, all users of the wiki can be held liable, because any of them had the ability to remove or amend the defamatory material from the "publication". It remains to be seen whether wikis will be regarded as more akin to an internet service provider, which is generally not held liable due to its lack of control over publications' contents, than a publisher.[7] It has been recommended that trademark owners monitor what information is presented about their trademarks on wikis, since courts may use such content as evidence pertaining to public perceptions, and they can edit entries to rectify misinformation.[59] Conferences Active conferences and meetings about wiki-related topics include: Atlassian Summit, an annual conference for users of Atlassian software, including Confluence.[60] OpenSym (called WikiSym until 2014), an academic conference dedicated to research about wikis and open collaboration. SMWCon, a bi-annual conference for users and developers of Semantic MediaWiki.[61] TikiFest, a frequently held meeting for users and developers of Tiki Wiki CMS Groupware.[62] Wikimania, an annual conference dedicated to the research and practice of Wikimedia Foundation projects like Wikipedia. Former wiki-related events include: RecentChangesCamp (2006–2012), an unconference on wiki-related topics. RegioWikiCamp (2009–2013), a semi-annual unconference on "regiowikis", or wikis on cities and other geographic areas.[63] See also icon Internet portal Comparison of wiki software – Software to run a collaborative wiki compared Content management system – Software for managing digital content CURIE – Form of abbreviated URI Dispersed knowledge – Information that is spread throughout the market and not in the hands of a single agent Fork and pull model – A common alternate paradigm List of wikis Mass collaboration – Many people working on a single project Universal Edit Button – Browser extension indicating a website is editable Wikis and education Notes The realization of the Hawaiian /w/ phoneme varies between [w] and [v], and the realization of the /k/ phoneme varies between [k] and [t], among other realizations. Thus, the pronunciation of the Hawaiian word wiki varies between ['wiki], ['witi], ['viki], and ['viti]. See Hawaiian phonology for more details.
